questions.scenario:
A design note for devpolaris-orders-api says:

workload: long-running Node.js HTTP API
package: container image in ECR
traffic: ALB forwards to port 3000
team goal: avoid host patching and SSH operations for this API

Which compute shape is the best first fit?

options.text: Run it as an ECS service on Fargate, with the image, task definition, service, health check, and logs as the main operating surface.
Run it on one EC2 instance because any service with an HTTP port must expose a virtual server to operators.
Run it as one Lambda function without changing the request model, because Lambda is just a hidden port listener.
Keep only the ECR image and skip a runtime service until traffic arrives.

questions.explanation: The evidence points to a containerized service that should stay alive behind a load balancer. ECS on Fargate keeps the service shape while AWS manages the host capacity. EC2 would add server work, and Lambda would require an event or request handler design rather than a normal long-running port listener.

questions.scenario:
A fraud-scoring side component has this support note:

requires vendor Linux agent on host
requires local file inspection under /var/lib/vendor
support team asks for VM-level logs during incidents
traffic is internal and low volume

Which first compute choice is most honest?

options.text: Use EC2 for this component, because the requirement is host-level control and the team must accept server operations with it.
Use Fargate and plan to install the vendor agent on the managed host after the task starts.
Use Lambda because the traffic is low volume, even though the vendor requires a host agent.
Keep it in the same ECS service as the orders API so every backend has one deployment surface.

questions.explanation: This is the kind of exception where EC2 can be the right answer. The useful clue is not traffic volume, it is host control. Fargate and Lambda deliberately move the host away from your operating boundary.

options.text: Lambda, because this is handler-shaped work that starts from an event and ends after processing that event.
EC2, because every background job needs a process manager even when it only reacts to events.
The existing ALB target group, because target groups execute small jobs when HTTP traffic is quiet.
A larger Fargate task for the checkout API, because side effects should stay inside the request path.

questions.explanation: The event is the important evidence. Lambda is a good fit when each run is tied to an invocation and can finish inside the function limits. Keeping slow side work inside the checkout path would make the API more fragile.

questions.scenario:
A runbook lists where failures appeared during three incidents:

EC2 incident: systemd inactive, disk 96 percent full, ALB target timeout
ECS incident: task stopped reason ResourceInitializationError, target unhealthy, CloudWatch startup log missing DATABASE_URL
Lambda incident: REPORT line status timeout, SQS message retried, duplicate receipt blocked by idempotency key

Which statements match the compute ownership boundary?

options.text: EC2 puts more server evidence in the team path, including service status, disk, OS logs, and instance access.
ECS on Fargate shifts host care away, but the team still owns task definitions, roles, health checks, and logs.
Lambda failures are usually read through invocation logs, duration, retries, event shape, and concurrency signals.
All three compute shapes should be debugged first by SSHing to the host and checking port 3000.

questions.explanation: The same app family can fail in different evidence streams depending on the compute shape. EC2 is server-shaped, ECS is service-shaped, and Lambda is invocation-shaped. A single SSH and port checklist misses the point of Fargate and Lambda.

questions.scenario:
A release review says:

image built: yes
ECS service updated: yes
target health: 0 healthy, 2 unhealthy
CloudWatch logs: DATABASE_URL missing
ALB response: 503 from awselb/2.0

What is the best correction to the statement "compute is the whole architecture"?

options.text: Compute is where the code runs, but the runtime also depends on configuration, secrets, IAM, health checks, networking, and logs.
Compute is only the Docker image, so target health and secrets are outside the release conversation.
Compute replaces IAM because a running task can read every secret by default.
Compute replaces networking because the app will receive public traffic once a process starts.

questions.explanation: The evidence shows a runtime contract, not just a place where code exists. The code can be built and the service can be updated while traffic still fails because config, secrets, or health checks are wrong. Naming those surrounding pieces makes debugging calmer.
